front: Figure: worked with Jose de San Martin to liberate most of South America from Spanish rule
Figure: led the independence movements of five countries, including one (Bolivia) named after him
Figure: his early revolutionary career followed that of "The Precursor," a Venezuelan revolutionary who canvassed European courts for support of Spanish American independence
work: Francisco de Miranda & Early Revolutionary Career

front: Figure: while visiting Rome with a professor, made a vow on the Monte Sacro to pursue the career path that would make him famous
work: Francisco de Miranda & Early Revolutionary Career

front: Event: his encounter with an older revolutionary colleague is commemorated today by a bilingual plaque at 58 Grafton Way in London
work: Francisco de Miranda & Early Revolutionary Career

front: Document: written in 1815 to the merchant Henry Cullen, arguing for the necessity of Spanish American unity
Document: lists six "sure conclusions," including that if any part of the world could revert to "primitive chaos," it would be their country in its last hour
Event: defeated by the royalist caudillo Jose Tomas Boves and his "Legion of Hell," which brought down the Second Republic of Venezuela
Figure: the failures of the earlier "Patria Boba" period pushed him toward a more conservative outlook, reflected in his 1815 letter written in exile
Event: 1813 military campaign that began in Cartagena and ended with the capture of Caracas, preceded by the Cartagena Manifesto
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Document: proclamation issued during the Admirable Campaign sanctioning the killing of Spanish resistance and exonerating murders of political enemies
back: Decree of War to the Death
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Event: the campaign's first victory came by taking the town of Ocana, followed by a siege of Cucuta
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Event: included battles at Niquitao and Los Horcones, aided by the guide Tinjaca
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Event: a general sent a spy to convince the enemy a rear attack was imminent, allowing his army to march up the banks of the Zulia River
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Figure: Colonel Girardot was shot planting a flag on Barbula Hill during this campaign; his heart was removed and placed in a silver urn at burial
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Event: early in the campaign, the victors gathered supplies at Mompox
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Figure: was given his dog during this 1813 campaign, which supposedly blocked the advancing army's path by barking
work: The Admirable Campaign & the Decree of War to the Death

front: Battle: won with Francisco de Paula Santander after a daring crossing of the Andes, securing New Granada's independence (August 1819)
work: The Battle of Boyaca & the Congress of Cucuta

front: Battle: this victory paved the way for the 1821 victory at Carabobo
work: The Battle of Boyaca & the Congress of Cucuta

front: Document: Gran Colombia's founding constitution, drafted by the Congress of Cucuta (1821), remained in force until the confederation's 1831 collapse
work: The Battle of Boyaca & the Congress of Cucuta

front: Event: was also the site of a meeting organized by Antonio Narino at the Villa del Rosario
work: The Battle of Boyaca & the Congress of Cucuta

front: Battle: 1821 defeat of the Royalist army commanded by Miguel de la Torre, securing Venezuela's independence
work: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela

front: Figure: nicknamed the "Centaur of the Plains," this chief subordinate drove the Spanish out of their last Venezuelan stronghold at Puerto Cabello
work: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela

front: Figure: served as aide-de-camp at the Battle of Carabobo
work: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela

front: Battle: Bolivar's dog Nevado died during this victory
work: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela

front: Figure: plains herders skilled at horsemanship who assisted Bolivar at Carabobo and Boyaca, nicknaming him "iron buttocks" (culo de hierro) for his endurance on horseback
work: The Battle of Carabobo & Liberation of Venezuela

front: Event: met Jose de San Martin here, just before becoming dictator of Peru
work: The Guayaquil Conference & Liberation of Peru and Bolivia

front: Event: his refusal to share command with Jose de San Martin here caused San Martin to lose the momentum he had gained and withdraw
work: The Guayaquil Conference & Liberation of Peru and Bolivia

front: Figure: alongside this lieutenant, Bolivar won Bolivia's independence; nicknamed the "Grand Marshal of Ayacucho," he succeeded Bolivar as the country's second president
work: The Guayaquil Conference & Liberation of Peru and Bolivia

front: Figure: proposed a government with a never-implemented tricameral legislature
work: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia

front: Figure: frequently clashed with a separatist rival over presidential powers before resigning
work: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia

front: Event: an 1828 constitutional convention held here (which Bolivar skipped, sending Daniel O'Leary as proxy) collapsed when the Santander faction walked out, leading Bolivar to declare himself dictator
work: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia

front: Event: the failure of an 1830 congress to halt Venezuelan separatism led to Gran Colombia's dissolution a year later
work: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia

front: Figure: this close friend and lieutenant of Bolivar was assassinated in 1830 by peons of a rival officer, and buried in Quito
work: Presidency, the Ocana Convention & Collapse of Gran Colombia

front: Figure: saved Bolivar from assassination during the 1828 Septembrine Conspiracy in Bogota, earning the nickname "Libertadora del Libertador"
Figure: in the 1828 conspiracy, led a group of mutinous officers on a wild goose chase, allowing her lover to escape out a window
Figure: met Bolivar in Quito after he liberated the Viceroyalty of New Granada
Figure: served as a nurse who may have fought in the Battle of Ayacucho, and often rode wearing a colonel's uniform
Figure: received the Order of the Sun for her role in Spanish American independence; first aided the movement by spying on guests in her English merchant husband's house
Figure: became known as the "Dame of the Sun" after receiving an award from Peru; later met Giuseppe Garibaldi during his tour of the Americas
Event: their many love letters survived only because Daniel O'Leary refused to burn Bolivar's papers after his death
Figure: after Bolivar's death, she was expelled from Colombia, forbidden from returning to her Ecuadorian birthplace, and died in poverty in Peru
Event: the socialism advocated by Hugo Chavez and Nicolas Maduro is named for this man
Event: a 1992 coup attempt was staged by a movement inspired by him called MRB-200; its leader later won election under the Fifth Republic Movement banner
Event: social programs like Mission Robinson (literacy) and Mission Mercal (hunger) are named for this man
Event: an international integration platform, originally called his "Alternative," was created in 2004 and proposed a virtual trade currency
Event: names Venezuela's currency and the "plurinational" country of Bolivia, which has two capitals
Figure: added an eighth star to Venezuela's yellow, blue, and red tricolor flag to represent Spanish Guayana
Event: M-19 guerrillas stole this man's sword
Event: a Venezuelan city renamed for him (formerly Angostura) created Angostura bitters
Event: interred at a national pantheon in 1883
Novel: depicts Bolivar's final, physically deteriorating river journey down the Magdalena, drawing partly on his own preserved late writings
back: The General in His Labyrinth (Gabriel Garcia Marquez)

front: Novel: in it, Bolivar learns Rafael Urdaneta has taken over the government and that his friend Sucre has been killed
back: The General in His Labyrinth (Gabriel Garcia Marquez)

front: Novel: Bolivar's entourage spends three days partying in Honda while he deteriorates to the point of being mistaken for his aide-de-camp Jose Palacios; enemies jeer "Sausage link!"
back: The General in His Labyrinth (Gabriel Garcia Marquez)

front: Novel: this erotic novel takes Bolivar's mistress as its subject
back: The Wife of Dr. Thorne (Denzil Romero)

front: Biography: a biography of Bolivar takes a chapter title from a novel using his quote "It seems that the devil controls the business of my life" as an epigraph
Novel: the character Pudge finds a line spoken by Bolivar, "straight and fast is the only way out," written in the margins
Figure: in memoirs about fighting alongside Bolivar, this man describes him as "a coward, a lecher, and incompetent"
